_meta.note: BuzzFeed quiz tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: My Emotional Resolution — How Many Pixels Do I Feel In?
meta_001: Some people feel a moment in broad strokes; others catch every subtle shade. Your emotional resolution, measured across 12 scenes. How many pixels do you feel in?
meta_002: Your emotional resolution across 12 scenes. From mosaic to 4K — how many pixels are you?
html_009: “hurt but envious, yet somehow at ease”
html_011: Same moment — how many pixels does your heart capture?
html_019: — the more you answer, the sharper it gets
html_029: 🧪 Dopamine Lab — more tests this way →
js_001: A friend you hadn’t seen in ages talked only about themselves. On the way home, your heart feels…?
js_003: Felt off. Guess I was tired.
js_004: Half hurt, half the regret that I never spoke up either. A little bit lonely.
js_005: Right before a big presentation, your heart races. What do you call this?
js_008: Half nerves, half excitement — the wish to do well hit my body first.
js_009: You see a friend’s good news online. What’s really behind your congrats comment?
js_011: Happy for them, but a little envious too.
js_012: Congrats, a faint jealousy, and a creeping impatience about my own life — three layers at once.
js_013: You lie down after a long day and, for no reason, your heart feels heavy. You…?
js_014: Just go to sleep. Some days are like that.
js_015: Take a moment to wonder why.
js_016: I hunt down what the heaviness actually is. That comment earlier? The thing I keep putting off? I name them one by one.
js_017: When someone asks “How are you feeling right now?”, you usually…?
js_018: Answer with one of: good / meh / not great.
js_019: Say good or bad, and tack on one line of why.
js_020: Answer by mixing two or three words, like “excited but a little uneasy.”
js_021: You got angry. If you look quietly into that anger?
js_022: Anger is anger. I’m just mad.
js_023: I can at least tell irritation from real fury.
js_024: I can see the texture — whether it’s feeling dismissed, or wronged, or let down.
js_025: When you listen to music you love, your emotion is?
js_027: It splits into upbeat songs / sad songs, roughly.
js_028: I feel subtle shades like “nostalgic yet warm,” or “lonely but at ease.”
js_029: How often do you use words that name emotions?
js_031: I often reach for words like hurt, proud, relieved.
js_032: Choked-up, wistful, tender, sheepish… I pick out fine-grained words.
js_034: Often just say “I don’t know, no reason.”
js_035: I try to explain, but the right words won’t come.
js_036: I can put my state into fairly precise words.
js_037: You watched a sad movie. What lingers after it ends?
js_038: Either I cried or I didn’t. One or the other.
js_039: It stays as “that was sad” or “that moved me.”
js_040: I can recall which scene, and exactly what nuance in it, touched me.
js_041: Do you link body signals (tight chest, lump in your throat) to emotions?
js_042: Body is body, mind is mind. I rarely connect them.
js_043: Sometimes I connect it as far as “oh, that’s stress.”
js_044: A tight chest means anxiety, a lump in the throat means grief… I read emotions through my body.
js_045: If you summed up a complicated day in a journal or a note?
js_047: I write mostly about what happened, event by event.
js_048: I can unspool the texture of each feeling as it came, in full sentences.
js_050: You feel emotions in big pixels. You capture the world in two or three settings — “good / bad.” In return, you decide fast and don’t stay rattled by small feelings.
js_051: You tend to sum up even complex feelings as “I’m fine” or “not great.” A simple heart means fewer grudges.
js_052: The strength not to be swallowed by emotion. While others get tossed around by tiny moods, you see the big picture.
js_053: When “I feel bad” rises up, try adding just one more word. Angry? Hurt? Worn out? One extra pixel already makes the world a little sharper.
js_055: You tell the basic emotions apart clearly — happy, sad, angry, anxious, all pinned down accurately. The subtle in-between colors still smudge, but your picture is plenty sharp.
js_056: You do name feelings — like “I felt a bit hurt today” — but when several overlap, you tend to lump them into one.
js_057: Someone who can put their feelings into words. Fewer misunderstandings in relationships, and honest when it counts.
js_058: Try letting go of the idea that a moment holds only one feeling. The instant you spot a *layered feeling* like “happy but wistful,” your resolution jumps.
js_059: Your heart runs sharp. You notice the texture of overlapping feelings and can usually explain why they’re there. You read the subtleties in yourself and others well.
js_060: You naturally feel double emotions like “hurt, but grateful anyway.” You tend to trace feelings back to their cause.
js_061: Delicate empathy. You read the texture even when the other person says nothing, and handle your own feelings with finesse. You have a gift for softening conflict.
js_062: Try keeping a *record* of the textures you feel. The habit of catching a moment’s subtlety in sentences is what carries you to 4K.
js_064: You feel emotions in ultra HD. Someone who catches even the finest gradients in language. You know the difference between wistful, choked-up, and sheepish, and you translate even body signals into feeling.
js_065: Even when three or four feelings stack up in one moment, you notice each and name it. Your emotional map is remarkably fine-grained.
js_066: Deep self-understanding and empathy. The wellspring of artistic sensitivity and delicate relationships. The people beside you feel truly “understood.”
js_067: Too sharp a picture can wear you out. Don’t try to interpret every feeling — every now and then, allow yourself a low-res mode that just *feels it and lets it pass*.
js_069: Halfway there! The picture’s coming into focus 🔍
jst_007: How many pixels is my emotional resolution?

_app.title: Emotional Resolution Test
_app.desc: How many pixels do you feel in? Measured across 12 scenes.
